speaker: interviewer
text: --Vertes, maiden name Katz. Today is January 31, 1996. My name is Michele Landa-Brooker. We're conducting this interview in Weston, Connecticut. And the interview language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, in-- uh, 1952 as a child, it was heartbreaking for me when I had to go and demonstrate and-- and-- and yell, long live Stalin and long live Rakosi, who was the equivalent-- Hungarian equivalent of Stalin. And inside, I-- I was very, very bitter that these people perhaps killed my father. But in 1953, fortunately Stalin died. And there was a general amnesty. And they, uh, let my father go. And my father was totally destroyed. He couldn't walk. He-- I don't know how he got home, because they just dropped him off at the corner. He was, uh, practically tortured to death. And it took him more than a year to recuperate. And, uh, then he just went back working with the Jewish congregation. And, um, in 1954, I graduated, uh, elementary school. And because I was a capitalist's child, an undesirable element, I could not-- could not have gone to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the Jewish high school in Budapest where all of us Jewish kids went who were undesirable elements. And it happened to have been a great school. And, uh, I had a great time there. I didn't have to worry about anti-Semitism or anything of that sort.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in, uh-- the next significant event in my life happened in 1956, which is the best thing that ever happened to me-- the Hungarian Revolution. And, uh, I was in-- I just started my third year in, uh, high school in, uh, September. And on October 23, I was on a street car, and, um, we were passing the, uh, offices of the, uh, publisher of the, uh, Hungarian communist newspaper. And I saw some leaflets flying down. And I, uh, said to myself, well, that's nothing unusual-- probably some communist propaganda. And when I got off the street car, I was going to get one of those leaflets. And some guy bumped into me. And he said to me, excuse me, Miss Citizen. That didn't sound-- that sounded very funny to me, because everybody was a 'comrade.' And now I am 'Miss Citizen.' Something is different here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was heading to a friend of mine. And, uh, I look at the leaflet. And it says, Russians go home. And wow, this is great. So I did my errand. And I went on the, uh, main ring that runs around the entire city, wide boulevard. It's a circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, it had trams-- excuse me-- with tracks and trolleys, and it's a wide street. And by the time I got there, the crowds were enormous. There-- there was no traffic going. Everything was at a standstill. The noise was deafening, people yelling, Ruskies, go home. And people who were on their way home, uh, they were, uh, sitting on these open trucks and yelling, we had enough of the Russians and et cetera, et cetera. And the crowds of people all over the street, and I knew that this was a revolution. It had to be. And some people who lived along those streets were hanging out the windows. They had box seats, you know. It was great. And then all of a sudden, I see that somebody, uh, unfurls a flag from one of the windows. And there is a hole in the middle. The Hungarian flag is red, white, and green-- red, white, and green. And in the middle, in the white, they had the communist symbol. And they cut it out. And that became the symbol of the revolution. And, uh, it was all very exciting and great. And I went home. And my mother didn't even know there was something going on. And, uh, so I said to her, um, I am going to go through the Stalin statue. Uh, there was, um, a big, um-- um-- what-- something like Place de la Concorde. And it had-- originally, it had monuments to the, uh, tribes of Hungary who came in-- in 896, each of them on a horse or whatever. [LAUGHS] And, uh, on one side, there was a church, which they destroyed. And in the place of it, they erected an enormous statue of Stalin-- our great father Stalin. And it was a-- a bronze statue. And I heard that they were going to take that down, because that was the symbol of oppression. And, um, so I said to my sister, uh, come, let's go. We're going to go down-- this was at the end of that boulevard, the Andrassy Boulevard, which was the Champs-Elysees-type boulevard. And it ended up like in the Place de la Concorde. Similarities were obvious. Uh, the city planner had the same thing in mind. Anyway, so we walked the length of that, uh, boulevard, which is maybe a mile and a half. And by the time we got there, the crowds were enormous. And everybody's screaming and yelling and-- and there was just such electricity in the crowd. And it was one of the most exhilarating moments in my life. And they were trying to, uh, get the statue down with torches. And they couldn't. Finally, they had to dynamite it. And, uh, the noise was just so deafening. And the crowds were like-- like waves, you know, like in an ocean, the wave hits you. And everybody is kind of getting hurled forward. And finally, they got the statue down. And I said to my sister, let's get out of here because we're going to get trampled. And they put out a rope around the-- the statute. And they attached it to some sort of vehicle. And they paraded it through the entire city, and people spit on it and, uh, did other things on it, too, that I'm not going to mention. And people chipped away at it. Everybody wanted a souvenir. And, uh, then we went home. And that night, we heard shots. My mother and I went outside. And we heard shots. And I knew it wasn't going to be a bloodless revolution.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next morning-- and my father, of course, was out of town, because that was his job. We didn't know where he was and when he's going to get home and what is going to happen. But the first thing, I'm going to go get bread, right? The first thing in the morning, like 6 or 7 o'clock, I run down, and I stand in line for bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hear all-- all of this shooting, all over. And also, I saw that some of the, uh, cobblestones were thrown up. The Russians were in there with their tanks already. And, uh, they tore up the street where I lived in. They came through with this huge tanks and-- on those narrow streets and tore up the pavement and everything. And they were sitting ducks in those tanks, because the kids were throwing Molotov cocktails at them. And, uh, more often than not, they were successful-- I mean, the kids. And those people just fried in there. But anyway, uh, then, uh, the next day I went to the radio station, because I knew that communications is important. [LAUGHS] And if they take over the radio, they won the revolution. They did take over the radio, but they did not win the revolution. And I wasn't there when they took over the radio, because I turned out to be chicken, because there was a lot of fighting and shooting going on. And, um, after a while, we knew that the Russians had gotten us again. And I said to my parents, I'm young, and I've had enough of this miserable country, where first I was persecuted as a Jew. Now I am of the wrong heritage otherwise, too. I am a descendant of the wrong people. I could never go to a university. I am getting the hell out of here. And my brother, unfortunately, was sick. He had, um, a-- a liver infection-- hepatitis perhaps. I don't know. They called it liver infection.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to them, I'm leaving. I know that, uh, you can't come because the doctor said you can't. But you're old. They were 10 years younger than I am now. You are old. And they're not going to want to pay you pension, and they'll let you out. But me, I am young. The whole world is ahead of me. Obnoxious little brat, huh? 16 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my father and my mother deliberated. And then they decided, OK, my father will take us down. Oh, because I said I want Susan to go with me. Susan came everywhere with me. You know, we belonged together. And, uh, they decided that my father will take this down by train to the border. And there, uh, we're going to get across somehow. We'll pay somebody who leads us across. So we went-- the next morning, we went to the railroad station. This is now sometime in November, and, uh-- November '56. And we went to the town of Sopron, which, uh, is on the border of, uh, Austria and Hungary. And, uh, we went with a group of people. My father paid some peasant that they were going to take us across. And, uh, we were almost across, or so we thought. And all of  a sudden, the Russians appear with their tanks. And, uh, they take us back a while-- not all the way back, just back a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, there is a young Russian officer on one of the tanks. And he starts yelling, who here speaks Russian? I didn't want to say anything, because I just wanted to be anonymous. [LAUGHS] And then he got really mad. Isn't there-- everybody is mute here! And isn't there anybody who speaks Russian? And then, finally, I raised my hand, and I said, I speak Russian. He says to  me, you, up here. So I go up. I'm standing on the tank. And I said, I have a sister with me. Get your sister! So I-- my sister and I were standing on the tank. And he says, here's what I want you to do. You tell me who is who, what they did. You translate for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. They packed up all the people, and they took them to jail. And he says to me, you and your sister stay here. And I am-- you know, I'm 16 years old. I'm worried that I'm not going to be raped or something. I'm really upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says to me, you and you, I've never seen you before. You go back to your parents. How can you do this to your parents? If I catch you next time, I'm going to put you in jail. [LAUGHS] So we got out of that. And one more time, we got caught. And the third time, it was already, uh-- there was another, uh, brother team. We were a sister team and a brother team that always got caught. They paired us up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And supposedly, this peasant really knew the way. But he said that this way is-- still has mines in it. That's why they don't patrol it. But he knows the way. And if we follow exactly in his footsteps, we're not going to get blown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you better believe, we followed exactly. And, um, the, uh-- one of the fellows was recently released from prison, a Jewish guy, a rabbi. Because he was a Zionist or something, he-- he was in prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we finally see soldiers. And we thought, oy, here we go again. We're getting caught. This is now the third or fourth time. And, uh, the guy says to us, the-- the-- leader, the, uh-- excuse me, the Hungarian guy says to us, no, don't worry. These are the Austrian border patrol. And indeed, they were. And this guy, who just was liberated from prison, he bent down and kissed the ground. And then I took out-- we had butter with us. And that-- we had no paper with us. We had a bar of butter. I took the paper off. And I wrote in Hebrew to my father to pay this man-- not to pay him. That was the arrangement, that if I write   in Hebrew that we are free, he's going to pay this guy. He wasn't going to get paid otherwise. And I did that. And the guy went back. And, uh, we went to this, uh-- they took us to a camp where this person, who was in prison before, he knew German. So he became the interpreter there. And I didn't like camps. And, uh, the social worker for whom he was interpreting promised him that he was going to take him into Vienna, because he had relatives there. And I said to him, you must. You must take us with you, because I am not staying in a camp. Camp to me was something I just definitely never wanted to stay in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said-- he said, OK, but you can't come to our relatives with us. I said, never mind. I'm not going to your relatives. Just take me to Vienna. So at midnight, we arrived in Vienna, my sister and I, 16 and 14, and no place to live, no place to go. There they are, the two of us, dazzled by the sight of Vienna, a Western city by then, because they were liberated. I mean, they became independent from the Russians earlier. I've never seen a Western city like that. I was totally dazzled. It didn't faze me at all that I had no place to sleep. And then, all a sudden, I hear my name. And a young man I went to school with, he said to me, what are you doing here? I look at him. I said, the same thing as you are doing. [LAUGHS] He says, how long have you been here? I said, about 10 minutes. He says, where do you live? I said, no place.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you can't do that, because they're going to put you in jail if they find you on the street-- for loitering. And he says, we're in a camp. I said, oh, no, no-- no camp for me. He says, listen, this is a horrible camp. But you have to stay there at night. Uh, he was with a friend. Peter and I will stay up and make sure that nobody rapes you. And we'll give you our coat to sleep on. And then tomorrow, you take care of your, uh, official stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what we did. And then the next day, we went to the Joint to register. And of course, they were closed for some holiday. Must have been Thanksgiving or something. I-- no, because I crossed in December. By the time I crossed the border,  it was December.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, so anyway, we still didn't have any place to stay. And we went to the-- to Weiburggasse, which is a-- which had a kosher restaurant. And most of the religious Jews, uh, tended to congregate there. And I saw people changing money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, um, some young men befriended me, from South America, as a matter of fact. And he bought me lunch. And, uh, he-- I said to him, what are those people doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, well, they're making money. They're changing dollars to, uh, shillings and shillings to Hungarian money and then back and forth. And they make a profit on each transaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I can do that, too. He says, how would you do that? You don't have any dollars. I said, you wanna bet? I had dollars, uh, in my, uh, coat lining. My mother sewed some dollars in so we're not penniless. And I started trading. And this is how I supported my sister and myself. And, uh, we by chance were taken in by a family, uh, to live with. Because I met a friend of my father's on the street, and he said to me, oh, where-- where's your father? I said, back home. He says, what are you doing here? Where are you living? I said, no place, on the street. And he said, oh, you can't do that. I tell you what. I'm going to call an employee of mine who used to run my Viennese, uh, store. And he's a very nice guide, not rich, but very nice. He'll take you in. And that's where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I, uh, found somebody who had a very sure way of, uh, getting people across the border. And it cost $400 for my parents and my brother. And I, uh, put the money down at a third party. I wouldn't give them the money. And I told them, when you bring my parents, you'll get the money. And again, I sent something in Hebrew to tell my parents that it is me, indeed, and it's not a trick. And they came. And when they arrived to that Weihburggasse restaurant, and I saw two crying people, whom I thought were going to take care of me, I was very disappointed, in a sense. But, uh, from then on, the-- the Joint took care of us. They gave us money to live in a hotel as a family. And, uh, my brother was in the hospital, where he also caught, uh, encephalitis. And I think that he eventually died of the complications from that. And I think that I'm going to carry the guilt with me to my grave that, uh, I forced them to come. And maybe he wouldn't have gotten that if they didn't come. And, uh, I had two uncles here in this country. One, my mother's brother, younger brother, the one who had the typhoid fever, who, after he recovered, he went to, uh, Germany and was in a DP and then came to this country. And my father's brother, who was the chemical engineer who became a busboy in California, he was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had, uh, priority. And, uh, as soon as my brother was better, we came, and we were, uh, taken to-- excuse me-- to Camp Kilmer, where, um, my uncle took us from. He lived in the Bronx at the time in a three-room apartment with his wife and his son.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we descended upon him-- five more people in a three-room apartment-- eight people. But they-- they were very happy to see us. And we lived with them for three weeks. And then, uh, we got an apartment near them. And, uh, my mother and father had a very rough time in this country because my brother was sick almost until the day he died. And, uh, my parents were heartbroken. And besides, they just couldn't make a decent living here. Because, uh, my father was a businessman, and he didn't have enough money to start his own business. He was too old to start anything. He was, uh, in 1957-- got here February 23, 1957. Uh, he was at that time 48 years old. And he couldn't really start over. And, uh, my mother worked in the, uh-- at Lillie Rubin. I don't know if you   know the store. Lillie Rubin is a fancy, uh, fashion store. And she was first, uh, sewing as a-- in the back room. And then she later became a supervisor and fitter and, I don't know, whatever. And they had a very hard time, very, very hard time. And I, uh-- I was, uh-- all my life I wanted to get an education. And I felt in this country I am going to be able to get it. But I didn't know anything about Ivy League or anything like that. And nobody took the time to explain anything to me. In fact, we arrived in February. I took them until the end of April or May to get me to a school. And, uh, I didn't speak English. And I had six weeks left of the school year. And I decided by the time the school year ends, I better speak English. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went home with a headache every single day, but I learned English. And they gave me an IQ test when I  arrived. This was a beauty. And since I didn't know English, I turned out to be an imbecile on the test. And the, uh, principal took me under her wings and said, these people are crazy. Why did they give you an IQ test?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, she took me around to all the department heads. And they tested me, uh, like they asked me-- they wrote down, for instance, what's the logarithm of 1,000. And I said 3, and they were flabbergasted that off the top of my head. It's not a big deal, but they thought it was so great. And so anyway, I learned English. And, uh, I, uh, was very proud that, uh, the next year on my, uh regents, on my English regents, I think I got 94 or 95, which, uh, I'm very proud of, because many American kids failed it. And, uh, as I said, no one gave me any advice on college. And a young man I met, uh, in the Bronx, lived near me, he was going to Hunter College. And said-- he gave me a name at Hunter College and said, go and see this person. She's the registrar, and she'll tell you how to get in. And that's how I ended up at Hunter College. And this is where I met my husband, at Hunter College. So that was a pure coincidence as well, because he only went there for one semester. And, uh, we were both invited to a dinner party by the dean of foreign students. And that's where we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any message you'd like to leave us with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a great deal of messages to leave you with. Um, first of all, I would like to say that I am very disturbed nowadays when every event is called a holocaust. For instance, what happened in Bosnia is called a holocaust. I'm-- it is a tragedy what happened in Bosnia. And I don't mean to take away from the significance of the event. But the Holocaust, what we went through, was different for many reasons. One of the reasons is that the Jews were not fighting over territory. The Bosnians-- they are both fighting over-- both the Bosnian, the Serbs, the Croatians-- they're fighting over land. They all say, this land belongs to me. The Jews had no such claim. They weren't fighting for anything. These people have weapons. We didn't. We-- there was no way to obtain them. If the Bosnians-- if any Bosnian could get away from the site of fighting, they could live in peace. We had no place to go because all of Europe was-- almost all of Europe was occupied by the Nazis. America didn't want us. England didn't want us. Nobody wanted us. We had no way out. And also, somehow history is a tableau of persecutions and all sorts of tragedies, like during the uh, Middle Ages there were pogroms. And even before that, there were the Crusades. And in the name of Jesus, lots and lots of people were killed. The pogroms were localized. So one village was totally wiped out maybe. But other villages remained. There was a way to get away. Somehow, the conqueror always massacred the vanquished. But this wasn't the same way. This was not the same way, because you couldn't get away from it. Becoming a Christian didn't help, because if the blood was not Aryan, if  it was not pure, even if your grandparents were Jewish, you were a Jew. The Nazis-- changing religion didn't mean anything, because the Nazis were racist. And they considered the Jews a race. It wasn't considered a religion. So changing your-- becoming a Catholic or Protestant didn't change anything, really. And you couldn't get away from it. And to my descendants, I want to leave a message. Against incredible odds, I have survived this horrible Holocaust. And I find it that it is my duty to continue the traditions of Judaism. And the only way I can do that is to have my children remain Jews. And that's why I'm so adamant about my children marrying Jews. As far as I'm concerned, my daughter Vivion, my son Roger, and my granddaughter Morissa are my personal revenge against Hitler, because the first targets of Hitler were the children, because the children are the future. And children-- and Hitler wanted to wipe us off the face of the Earth. And if my children do not marry within the faith, we're going to disappear, and posthumously, Hitler will have won. And one more thing I want to say is that-- recently, somebody made a comment that really bothered me-- and it was a Jewish person, too-- that the Holocaust was no different-- what Hitler did to the Jews was no different than what we did to the blacks in the 1950s. Well, it was-- I think that's very wrong, because no-- no black person would be alive now since they're easily distinguishable. They couldn't hide like I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But also, there is the difference between the Bosnian conflict, the way the blacks were treated, and other present-day conflicts and the Holocaust. It was the first time in history that it wasn't only condoned to kill Jews, but it was the policy of the government. And they built an entire industry with the most modern technology just for the purpose of exterminating Jews. Why? Their only guilt was that they were Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for my descendants, one more thing-- our parents had thought that because they were living in this country, wherever they were, in Hungary, in France, or whatever, they were great patriots. And this is their country, and they were confident in their neighbors, and they led a good life. They couldn't believe that in a civilized society something like the Holocaust could happen. I do not recommend paranoia for my heirs. But I think that you should always be circumspect. And you should always look around you for signs of anti-Semitism and always champion the cause of a just society where racism doesn't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing that with us.
